recommended_datasets.reason: Orkut is a real-world social graph with ~3.0B edges and ~3M nodes — among the largest publicly available static graphs, directly aligned with LPS-GNN’s focus on 100-billion-edge scalability (even if still 2 orders of magnitude smaller, it's the closest benchmark for systems-level evaluation of neighbor explosion and memory pressure).

Friendster has ~1.8B edges and ~65M nodes — one of only a handful of real-world graphs approaching billion-edge scale; historically used for large-graph algorithm/systems validation, making it highly relevant for evaluating LPS-GNN’s deployment claims.

DBLP (up to 42.4M vertices, 400M triples) is a knowledge graph at the upper bound of commonly used large-scale GNN benchmarks; while 3 orders of magnitude below 100B edges, it serves as a meaningful intermediate-scale testbed for partitioning and low-memory message passing.

YAGO matches DBLP in scale (~42.4M vertices, 400M triples); as a dense knowledge graph, it stresses relational aggregation and schema-aware sampling — relevant for evaluating LPS-GNN’s flexibility on heterogeneous, high-degree graphs.

Yelp is explicitly cited for METIS-based partitioning evaluation in scalable GNN literature; though likely sub-billion in edge count, its real-world heterogeneity (user-business-review interactions) tests LPS-GNN’s adaptability to irregular topologies.

Explicitly named in the abstract as a core programming competition dataset used for training/evaluating program synthesis models; directly anchors the paper’s critique of current benchmarks and motivates its bootstrapping-for-repair approach.

Explicitly cited alongside MBPP in the abstract as a limiting benchmark; widely used in self-repair and iterative synthesis studies, making it foundational to the paper’s repair-centric bootstrapping methodology.
Standard real-world APR benchmark with compiler-validated bug fixes; directly supports the paper’s emphasis on human-like, iterative, compiler-assisted repair — a core conceptual pillar.

Evaluates functional correctness of autonomous fixes on real-world codebases; aligns tightly with the paper’s goal of grounding bootstrapped repair in realistic, test-driven, self-contained software contexts.
Enriched extension of MBPP (a named anchor dataset); adds test cases and descriptions to improve robustness evaluation of bootstrapped repair behavior — high relevance by derivation and purpose.

Directly supports both core contributions of DuPLUS: universal medical image segmentation (multi-parametric MRI with expert segmentation masks) and prognosis (explicit survival time prediction task), enabling end-to-end evaluation of the dual-prompt mechanism on clinically meaningful outcomes.

Explicitly designed for survival prediction using CT/PET + EHRs, making it uniquely suited to validate DuPLUS’s prognostic capability in a multimodal, text-conditioned oncology imaging framework—precisely matching the paper’s dual-segmentation-and-prognosis objective.

Massive scale (1.65M medical image-caption pairs) provides ideal pre-training signal for DuPLUS’s dual-prompt vision-language architecture, directly addressing the paper’s emphasis on scaling medical semantic understanding across domains for universal generalization.

Large-scale chest X-ray + radiology report pairs enable robust vision-language alignment and text-controlled semantic conditioning—critical for both segmentation guidance and report-informed prognosis, aligning with DuPLUS’s hierarchical prompt design.

Multimodal (imaging-adjacent pathology reports, genomics, survival outcomes) and pan-cancer scope makes it ideal for training/evaluating DuPLUS’s hierarchical dual-prompt architecture on semantic-guided segmentation and cancer prognosis.

MMBench is a comprehensive, high-quality multimodal benchmark explicitly designed for evaluating advanced cognitive capabilities in MLLMs—including reasoning, grounding, and generalization—making it the gold-standard evaluation suite for test-time warmup methods targeting holistic multimodal reasoning improvements.

Directly targets abstract visual reasoning on real-world images—a core challenge where MLLMs underperform and where test-time warmup could induce rapid, instance-adaptive conceptual refinement; its open-world design aligns with the paper’s emphasis on generalizable reasoning beyond narrow supervision.

Evaluates six fine-grained vision-language reasoning capabilities using LLM-based automated evaluation—ideal for quantifying nuanced gains from test-time warmup without human annotation bias, and directly supports the paper’s goal of boosting reasoning at inference time.

Extends Bongard-style abstraction to human-object interaction in real-world scenes—highly challenging, compositional, and grounded, matching the paper’s focus on enhancing complex, contextual multimodal reasoning during test-time adaptation.

Public multimodal math reasoning benchmark explicitly cited for MLLM evaluation; its formal problem structure and visual-text integration make it ideal for measuring whether test-time warmup improves logical consistency and stepwise reasoning under visual constraints.

Explicitly named in the paper's abstract as a core few-shot classification benchmark used to evaluate both Hebbian and gradient-based plasticity—direct experimental validation with evidence-backed record ID and maximal relevance to in-sequence adaptation.

Explicitly named alongside CIFAR-FS in the abstract as a primary few-shot classification task for evaluating plasticity mechanisms; foundational in meta-learning literature and strongly aligned with rapid task adaptation goals.

Standard few-shot vision benchmark closely related to CIFAR-FS and Omniglot; evidence-backed with citation to same paper, supporting its use in plasticity evaluation for rapid adaptation across visual categories.

Large-scale, multi-domain few-shot benchmark explicitly designed for generalization across tasks—highly relevant to the paper’s emphasis on robust, cross-task in-context adaptation, despite lack of direct citation (no_evidence but strong conceptual fit).

CIFAR-100–derived few-shot benchmark; structurally identical to CIFAR-FS in evaluation protocol and domain—strong proxy for few-shot visual adaptation, with high alignment to paper’s experimental paradigm.

Directly addresses the paper's core critique of single-step task bias with multi-turn, real-world web interactions and dynamic adaptability—ideal for statistically grounded compute allocation analysis in long-horizon LLM web agent training.

Explicitly designed for complex, cross-domain web tasks with action sequences and real DOM/page context—perfectly aligned with the paper’s focus on multi-step web interaction and open-source LLM web agent evaluation.

Large-scale GUI agent benchmark with human-annotated action sequences and screenshots; supports visual grounding, multi-step planning, and SFT/RL training—highly relevant for statistical diagnosis of training dynamics and hyperparameter sensitivity.

Simulated web environment requiring low-level keyboard/mouse actions; enables controlled, compute-efficient RL/SFT experimentation and hyperparameter analysis—directly supports the paper’s goal of diagnosing compute allocation in web-agent training.

Multi-environment, multi-turn benchmark including dedicated web browsing tasks; explicitly built for evaluating open-ended, interactive LLM agents—strong match for statistical diagnosis of training behavior across realistic web workflows.

Explicitly designed for cross-lingual summarization in Indic languages (e.g., Hindi, Tamil, Marathi), which are canonical less-resourced languages; directly supports experimental evaluation of zero-shot, fine-tuning, and data augmentation approaches in low-resource settings.

Contains professionally annotated article-summary pairs across 45 languages—including Swahili, Bengali, Urdu, and other less-resourced languages—making it the broadest and most authoritative multilingual summarization benchmark aligned with the paper’s core focus.

Covers 18 languages including Swahili, Indonesian, Hindi, and Vietnamese; explicitly built for cross-lingual abstractive summarization and widely used for low-resource transfer experiments—strong match for multilingual fine-tuning and LLM translation pipeline evaluation.

Multilingual news summarization dataset covering French, German, Spanish, Russian, and Turkish—spanning varying resource levels (e.g., Turkish and Russian are medium-to-lower resourced); directly supports comparative analysis of fine-tuned mT5 and transfer approaches.

Hebrew abstractive summarization dataset (10k samples); while Hebrew is mid-to-high resourced, it is *less*-resourced than English in NLP tooling and pretraining coverage, and is occasionally treated as a target for low-resource adaptation—marginally relevant but included due to scarcity of alternatives in this tier.
Directly evaluates medical reasoning using USMLE-style questions—core to the paper's healthcare domain focus and explicit contrast between specialized vs. general-purpose LLMs.
Clinical VQA dataset used to benchmark multimodal specialized models (e.g., LLaVA-Med), aligning precisely with the paper’s emphasis on healthcare + multimodal specialization beyond fine-tuning.

Comprehensive multimodal benchmark spanning 12 dimensions and image/video modalities—strong match for the paper’s cross-domain multimodal specialization theme in healthcare, technical, and professional contexts.

Explicitly designed for financial reasoning and QA; evidence-backed with RAG support and record_id; directly supports the paper’s finance domain analysis and evaluation of real-world professional limitations.

Legal-domain multiple-choice dataset for identifying case holdings—perfectly matches the paper’s legal domain focus and need for domain-specialized evaluation, despite lacking evidence ID (reason is domain-specific and task-aligned).

Explicitly designed for evaluating LLM agents in multi-turn, open-ended, interactive settings across eight diverse environments (e.g., OS, DB, puzzles), enabling controlled study of decentralized coordination, failure modes, and adversarial behavior—directly aligned with Byzantine-robust decentralized coordination of LLM agents.

MARL benchmark with explicit multi-agent interaction, sparse rewards, and environment-level coordination; supports simulation of Byzantine agents and decentralized control—highly suitable for robust coordination research under adversarial conditions.

Directly evaluates truthfulness and resistance to hallucination—core vulnerabilities exploited by Byzantine agents in consensus settings—making it essential for assessing collective accuracy degradation under malicious influence.

Dynamic MARL environment supporting cooperative/competitive multi-agent interaction; structured observation-action space enables modeling of Byzantine agents and leader failure—strong relevance for decentralized coordination robustness evaluation.
Specifically targets hallucination detection—a primary attack vector for Byzantine agents—enabling evaluation of whether decentralized coordination mitigates or amplifies hallucinatory consensus.

Explicitly designed to probe medical LLM reasoning failure modes (e.g., Einstellung effect) with adversarial clinical scenarios—directly stress-tests Reason2Decide’s robustness, rationale fidelity, and exposure bias mitigation in clinical decision contexts.

Part of ERASER benchmark; provides human-annotated rationales for medical interventions—ideal for rationale extraction and classification, directly supporting Reason2Decide’s dual-task rationale+prediction alignment objective.

Provides physician-annotated clinical sentence entailment labels *with rationales* from MIMIC-III—enables rationale-driven classification, matching Stage-2 joint learning goals; clinical domain and annotation quality are highly aligned.

63k+ real-world medical dialogues across departments—supports rationale generation in conversational clinical decision-making, offering a complementary modality (dialogue) to structured EHRs and enhancing ecological validity.

Biomedical QA requiring yes/no/maybe answers grounded in PubMed abstracts; includes human-annotated rationales in some versions—strong clinical grounding and rationale-label pairing for self-rationalization training.
Explicitly named in the paper's abstract as one of the four datasets used to evaluate contrastive ECOC for adversarial defense — direct empirical relevance and evidence-backed.

Standardized adversarial robustness benchmark integrating CIFAR-10/100 and ImageNet subsets; cited in related work as a gold-standard platform for evaluating the robustness claims central to the paper.

Highly plausible among the 'four datasets' due to its canonical role alongside CIFAR-10 in ECOC and adversarial robustness literature; fine-grained classes stress codebook design, aligning with paper’s focus.
Canonical large-scale multiclass benchmark; paper’s emphasis on scalability, generalization, and adversarial robustness strongly implies use of ImageNet or a subset (e.g., ImageNet-100).

Foundational digit classification dataset widely used in ECOC and adversarial defense research; simplicity enables ablation and codebook analysis — highly plausible as one of the four evaluation datasets.

Directly named in the paper as a core platform (alongside Facebook and X); contains long-form, clinically annotated mental health posts from subreddits (e.g., r/depression), enabling hierarchical n-gram encoding and domain-aware attention with clinical grounding—perfect match for AttentionDep’s architecture and explainability goals.

Explicitly depression- and anxiety-focused forum dataset with expert severity annotations; supports ordinal depression assessment and explainable modeling via naturalistic text—strong alignment with AttentionDep’s domain-aware, explainable severity estimation objective.

Not a training dataset but a structured commonsense knowledge graph; highly relevant as a candidate external domain knowledge source for AttentionDep’s cross-attention mechanism to inject mental health concepts (e.g., 'hopelessness', 'anhedonia')—directly supports the 'domain-aware' and 'explainable' design claims.

Large-scale, Reddit-native dataset with fine-grained emotion labels including depression-relevant states (e.g., 'sadness', 'grief'); supports attention-based interpretability and contextual modeling—though lacks explicit clinical severity scores, its scale and platform origin make it valuable for pretraining or auxiliary supervision.

Contains empathetic, mental-health-adjacent dialogues with emotion and situation labels; useful for modeling affective context and interpersonal expression patterns relevant to depression discourse—moderately supportive for attention calibration and explanation generation despite lacking severity labels.

Contains real multichannel audio recordings with time-aligned DOA and distance estimates for up to 3 overlapping sources; explicitly supports 3D sound event localization and tracking — directly enables modeling and evaluation of auditory motion trajectories, making it the strongest empirical match for AMPBench's goal of probing motion perception deficits in LALMs.

Provides real-world multichannel binaural recordings synchronized with motion-capture ground truth for speaker positions over time; enables inference of velocity, direction, and trajectory — highly aligned with 'spatial blind spot' evaluation, especially for dynamic source localization and motion reasoning.

Designed for SELD with frame-level DOA annotations across multiple classes and spatial configurations; supports static localization robustly and offers temporal sequences suitable for motion trajectory analysis if velocity/direction is inferred — strong relevance, though lacks explicit motion labels (e.g., speed or acceleration).

Predecessor to STARSS23 with multichannel audio and DOA estimation, but missing distance estimation and explicit multi-source motion support; still valuable for baseline motion-aware SELD evaluation, though less comprehensive than STARSS23.

Contains videos with 3D audio metadata and spatial sound generation targets; small-scale but purpose-built for spatial audio tasks including binaural rendering and positional reasoning — uniquely bridges generative and perceptual spatial audio evaluation, relevant for probing LALM motion understanding.

Large-scale, real-world graph explicitly used for node-level anomaly detection (NAD) benchmarks; directly cited in NAD literature and ideal for evaluating Janus’s joint Euclidean/hyperbolic GNNs due to its heterophilic structure, rich features, and ground-truth node anomalies.

Standard NAD benchmark with verified node-level anomaly labels, heterogeneous features, and structural complexity; used in UniGAD — a unified NAD framework — confirming strong compatibility with multi-space GNN architectures like Janus.

Organic, real-world social graph with ground-truth node anomalies and sparse, heterophilic topology; widely adopted in unsupervised NAD research, making it highly suitable for validating Janus’s contrastive dual-space alignment.

Co-purchase graph explicitly used for node-level graph anomaly detection; cited in work combining hyperbolic neural networks with NAD — direct methodological alignment with Janus’s hybrid geometric representation goal.

Social network graph with known node-level anomaly labels and used in recent hyperbolic NAD studies; high topical relevance for evaluating hyperbolic + Euclidean joint modeling on small-world, hierarchical graphs.

Directly supports 2D-guided cross-modal alignment: large-scale real-world LiDAR-camera synchronization, BEV annotations, precise extrinsic calibration metadata, and widespread use in evaluating projection-aware fusion for 3D detection—fully aligned with paper's core problem of misalignment due to calibration errors.

High-fidelity, massive-scale real-world LiDAR-camera sequences with rigorous calibration, diverse urban scenarios, and rolling shutter modeling—ideal for robustness evaluation under realistic projection errors and extrinsic inaccuracies central to the paper’s motivation.

1,000+ synchronized LiDAR-camera sequences with rich 3D bounding boxes, full calibration parameters, and scene diversity—excellent for validating 2D-guided alignment strategies and quantifying sensitivity to projection errors in BEV fusion.

Foundational real-world dataset with LiDAR-camera pairs, 3D object annotations, and published extrinsics—enables controlled study of projection misalignment and 2D-guided supervision despite smaller scale and older sensor setup.

Corruption-augmented extension of nuScenes featuring eight realistic perturbations—including extrinsic calibration shifts—making it uniquely suited for benchmarking robustness of 2D-guided alignment under precisely the projection errors highlighted in the paper.

ALFRED is the canonical benchmark for embodied visual reasoning with natural language instructions in egocentric simulated environments, directly matching CLiViS's core problem: mapping free-form linguistic instructions to spatiotemporal action sequences grounded in dynamic egocentric video.

Explicitly designed to evaluate embodied spatial understanding in LVLMs using egocentric scenes and six spatial relationships—precisely targets CLiViS’s cognitive map objective and linguistic-visual synergy in spatial reasoning.

Features instruction-guided navigation in continuous, realistic 3D environments with unconstrained agent movement—strongly aligns with CLiViS’s emphasis on free-form instructions and long-horizon egocentric video reasoning.

Benchmarks open-vocabulary spatial understanding and episodic memory via human-generated questions in real 3D environments—directly supports CLiViS’s cognitive map and embodied reasoning evaluation goals.

Foundational VLN benchmark with human-annotated, egocentric navigation instructions in simulated houses—ideal for evaluating instruction following, semantic grounding, and spatiotemporal reasoning in dynamic indoor settings.

Explicitly designed for non-i.i.d. FL with realistic client heterogeneity and data partitioning; directly enables evaluation of how large global batches interact with statistical diversity — central to the paper’s core trade-off analysis between parallelism and statistical performance.

Standard, widely adopted image classification benchmark in FL literature; frequently used to study convergence, generalization, and communication-efficiency under large-batch training — perfectly aligned with the paper’s focus on large-batch FL trade-offs.
Specifically engineered for large-scale FL experiments; its extended size supports rigorous large-batch ablation studies, making it highly targeted to the paper’s titular emphasis on 'large-batches'.

Higher-class diversity and complexity than CIFAR-10; commonly used in FL to assess statistical efficiency and generalization under larger batch sizes and non-i.i.d. settings — strongly reinforces the paper’s empirical scope.

Bridges scale and realism between CIFAR and ImageNet; suitable for evaluating large-batch FL behavior in more challenging vision tasks without full ImageNet resource overhead — excellent balance of relevance and practicality.

Directly matches MAFA's enterprise-scale, financial-domain deployment: 2.9M real-world customer utterances from a financial VPA, explicitly designed for intent-slot annotation—precisely the backlog elimination use case described in the paper. Evidence-backed with record_id and highest reason score.

Fine-grained banking-domain intent classification (77 intents) aligns perfectly with MAFA’s target domain (financial services), task type (configurable intent annotation), and production needs. Though no-evidence, its description strongly supports relevance to MAFA’s configurable, domain-specific adaptation.

Standard benchmark for intent classification and slot filling on voice assistant queries—directly reflects MAFA’s core annotation functionality (configurable, structured, utterance-level). Domain proximity (user utterances) and task fidelity make it highly relevant despite being generic.

150-intent classification with out-of-scope detection mirrors MAFA’s production requirements: handling emerging utterance types dynamically. Its scale and OOS robustness evaluation align with MAFA’s consensus-driven, adaptable framework.

Semantic frame annotation (e.g., 'Transfer-Money' with roles) exemplifies the rich, structured, domain-configurable labeling MAFA enables—beyond flat intents, supporting predicate-argument structure critical in financial dialogue understanding.

BRAX is a differentiable physics simulator explicitly designed for backpropagation through time (BPTT), directly enabling reparameterization policy gradients (RPG) — the paper’s core technical mechanism. Its tight alignment with computing PPO-style surrogate objectives via differentiable dynamics makes it the most relevant dataset for implementation and evaluation.

DMCS provides standardized, smooth, continuous-control environments with analytic dynamics and MuJoCo backends — ideal for isolating and validating RPG–PPO surrogate stability. Its design supports both theoretical analysis and empirical benchmarking of gradient variance reduction in differentiable settings.

MuJoCo is the canonical physics-based continuous-control benchmark used extensively in PPO, RPG, and differentiable RL literature. Its high-fidelity, differentiable (via finite-difference or custom adjoints) dynamics align precisely with the paper’s focus on sample-efficient, stable policy optimization via reparameterized surrogates.

NeoRL provides conservative offline MuJoCo datasets, enabling rigorous evaluation of the paper’s claim about stable sample reuse under distributional shift — a direct testbed for the proposed reparameterized PPO surrogate objective in data-constrained regimes.

RL Unplugged offers large-scale, high-quality offline MuJoCo datasets, supporting empirical validation of surrogate objective robustness and reuse efficiency — central to the paper’s stability argument bridging RPG and PPO.

Explicitly designed for QA over long scientific documents with fine-grained evidence span annotation—directly targets ECoRAG’s core innovation: evidentiality-guided compression and sufficiency verification in long-context RAG.

Provides claims with annotated supporting evidence and veracity labels—ideal for evaluating whether ECoRAG preserves *claim-supporting evidence* during compression, aligning precisely with its evidential sufficiency checking mechanism.

Purpose-built for extended multi-document QA under strict long-context constraints—matches ECoRAG’s problem scope, evaluation design, and need for cross-document evidential compression without loss of support.

Requires explicit multi-hop evidence identification and reasoning; widely used to test whether compressed contexts retain sufficient supporting evidence—directly validates ECoRAG’s iterative retrieval and evidence-preserving compression.

Demands chaining of distributed evidence across multiple hops—rigorously tests whether ECoRAG preserves *all necessary evidential links*, not just surface facts, making it highly aligned with the framework’s evidential fidelity goal.
